title: The Crumbs Beneath the Table
subtitle: A Canaanite woman cries after Christ for her tormented daughter, and will not be sent away

stillness: Be still, and leave behind the borders of your sins.
To go out from the borders of sinners is to cut away the causes of sin,
and to give their whisperings no opening.
Come away, then, even from the occasions — and wait here, quietly, for mercy.
gospel: Jesus went out from there and withdrew into the parts of Tyre and Sidon.
And behold, a woman of Canaan came out from those borders,
and cried out, saying: Have mercy on me, Lord, Son of David;
my daughter is grievously vexed by a demon.
But he answered her not a word.
And his disciples came and besought him, saying: Send her away, for she crieth after us.
He answering said: I was not sent but to the sheep that are lost of the house of Israel.
But she came and adored him, saying: Lord, help me.
He said: It is not good to take the bread of the children and to cast it to the dogs.
But she said: Yea, Lord; for the whelps also eat of the crumbs that fall from the table of their masters.
behold: This woman had heard the fame and the miracles of Jesus;
therefore she firmly believed that he could heal her daughter.
See how she cries out: Have mercy on me — for I am wretched.
In that one word, mine, the vehemence of her love is laid bare:
the evil of another, her daughter's, she counts as her own.
She alleges no merit, she asks no judgment, she does not forget her own defect;
she begs only a kindness, and so: Have mercy on me.
And to move him more to compassion, she pours out to him her whole grief:
My daughter — not a servant, not a stranger, but a daughter — is grievously vexed,
and not by any pain, but by a demon, which is more perilous still.
To the physician she uncovers her wounds, the greatness and the kind of the disease.
But he answered her not a word — not from anger, but to prove her constancy,
and from her perseverance in prayer to show her faith.
Behold her humility: rejected, she does not blush, but cries the more insistently,
and adored him as the Saviour, with the adoration of latria, saying: Lord, help me.
Behold her humility, which is magnified the more, the greater the rebuke was:
for she was called not daughter, not worthy of bread, but rather a dog —
and she granted it all, saying: Yea, Lord.
As if she said: True is thy word, for I am a dog.
Thou callest me a dog; thy dog I wish to be — feed then thy dog,
for I cannot leave the table of my Lord.
Masters do not drive out their own dogs;
and if they are driven out by one door, they return by another.
If then I am a dog, thou must not drive me from thee;
and if thou drive me from thee, at once I will come back to thee.
consider: She calls him Lord, because of the divine nature, and Son of David, because of the human;
she confesses the true God and the true man in one.
Where she says Lord, she believes him God; where she says Son of David, she believes him man.
A short word — Have mercy on me — but it holds within it a measureless sea of kindness;
for where mercy is asked, there all good things are contained together.
She runs to mercy, where judgment ceases; she runs to mercy, where unspeakable salvation is.
It is not enough for a man to turn himself from his sins,
unless he also go out from the borders of sin — the occasions and the causes of sinning.
Lord, I do not ask the whole loaf; I ask not bread, but a crumb.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, I ask thee and beseech thee, have mercy on me,
and help me in my necessities, and in the vexations of temptations and tribulations.
And if, Lord, because I am a dog, and before thee scarcely even as a dog,
I am not worthy to receive from thee the whole bread — the greatness of thy gifts —
at least give me the little crumbs: some small portion of thy grace,
without which my soul is grievously vexed by the demon;
but with which, however small it be, it shall be set free from the demon.
For thy grace, dwelling in the soul, blots out all sins,
and makes the soul an adopted daughter to God. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Modicus sermo, sed immensum benignitatis continet pelagus; ubi enim misericordia postulatur, ibi universa comprehensa sunt bona.
latinJewel.english: A short word, but it holds within it a measureless sea of kindness; for where mercy is asked, there all good things are contained together.
